Title: A concise note on machine learning evaluation

This teaching note discusses machine learning evaluation. It will explain why measurement is difficult and list the assumptions that make the analysis tractable. The main point is that a reliable conclusion requires both context and measurement. For example, a system may look effective under one benchmark but behave differently when the input distribution changes. A careful analysis therefore separates the mechanism, the data, and the evaluation metric. In practice, this separation helps

Theorem. Suppose a sequence is bounded and monotone. Then the sequence converges. Proof. Because the sequence is bounded, it has an upper bound. By the completeness property of the real numbers, the set of its values has a least upper bound. We show that the distance between the sequence and this bound becomes arbitrarily small.  We then check the boundary cases and verify that the units are consistent. If the logarithm is natural, the entropy is measured in

Problem (probability). Let X be a discrete random variable with probabilities proportional to 2, 11, and 13. Compute the normalized probabilities and then express the entropy in natural units. Solution. First compute the total mass Z = 2 + 11 + 13. The probabilities are obtained by dividing each mass by Z. The entropy is the negative sum of p times log p over all possible outcomes.

Problem (probability). Let X be a discrete random variable with probabilities proportional to 9, 15, and 24. Compute the normalized probabilities and then express the entropy in natural units. Solution. First compute the total mass Z = 9 + 15 + 24. The probabilities are obtained by dividing each mass by Z. The entropy is the negative sum of p times log p over all possible outcomes.

Problem (statistics). Let X be a discrete random variable with probabilities proportional to 7, 8, and 15. Compute the normalized probabilities and then express the entropy in natural units. Solution. First compute the total mass Z = 7 + 8 + 15. The probabilities are obtained by dividing each mass by Z. The entropy is the negative sum of p times log p over all possible outcomes. Optimization example. We minimize L(w) = (w - 7)^2 + 6/10. The gradient is 2(w - 7), so the stationary point is w = 7. Since the second derivative is positive, this stationary point is the unique minimizer. A gradient descent algorithm with a small step size will move toward this value.
